آلودگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آلُودَگِی (ālūdagī). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi آلودگی (ālvdgy /⁠ālūdagī⁠/).

آلودہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آلوده (ālūda), past participle of آلودن (ālūdan, “to pollute”).

آلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آلَه (āla), from Arabic آلَة (ʔāla).

آم.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀩 (aṃba), from Sanskrit आम्र (āmrá).
Borrowed from Sanskrit आम (āma). Doublet of خام (xām).
Borrowed from Sanskrit आम (āma). Doublet of خام (xām).

آمدنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آمَدَنِی (āmadanī), from آمَدَن (āmadan, “to come, arrive”).

آمر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آمر (āmir), from Arabic آمِر (ʔāmir).

آملہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آمله (āmla). Doublet of آن٘وْلا (ā̃vlā).

senses.g: a female given name, Amina, from Arabic

آموختہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آموخته (āmoxta), past participle of آموختن (âmoxtan, “to teach; learn”). Previously attested in 1649.

senses.g: a learned lesson; trained (a lesson which has been completed)
something which has been used to learn or read from.

آموختہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آموخته (āmoxta), past participle of آموختن (âmoxtan, “to teach; learn”). Previously attested in 1649.

آمیزش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آمیزش (āmēziš). Cognate with Bengali আমেজ (amej), a Persian borrowing.

آنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀆𑀯𑁂𑀇 (āvei), from Sanskrit आपयति (āpayati), from आप् (āp) + -अयति (-ayati). The perfective form آیا (āyā) may be derived from Sanskrit आगत (ā́gata, “arrived”).

senses.g: A middle-aged woman who easily engages in sexual relationships (especially with younger men).
A woman considered unattractive or unworthy of attention due to her age; middle-aged and unappealing woman.
Courtesan, sex worker, or a woman of questionable behavior.
pallu, aanchal, the end of a sari

آنچل.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀘𑀮 (aṃcala), from Sanskrit अञ्चल (añcala).

senses.g: Andhra Pradesh (a state in southeastern India, where Telugu is the primary language)
storm (ie. tempest, cyclone or a storm of any kind of rain, hail, snow etc.)

آندھی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi آندھی (āndhy /⁠āndhī⁠/) (c. 1530), from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit अन्धिका (andhikā), a compound of अन्ध (andha) + इका (ikā).

senses.g: vocative plural of آنِسَہ (ānisa, “Miss – the title of an unmarried woman”)
oblique plural of آنِسَہ (ānisa, “Miss – the title of an unmarried woman”)
direct plural of آنِسَہ (ānisa, “Miss – the title of an unmarried woman”)

ex.u: اُس کی آن٘کھوں میں آن٘سُو بَھر آئے۔
ex.r: us kī ā̃khõ mẽ ā̃sū bhar āe.
ex.e: Her eyes were filled with tears.

آنسو.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀲𑀼 (aṃsu), from Sanskrit अश्रु॑ (áśru), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Háćru, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂eḱru, from *h₂eḱ-. Cognates include Gujarati આંસુ (ā̃su), Marathi आसू (āsū), Nepali आँसु (ā̃su), Punjabi ہنجھو / ਹੰਝੂ (hañjhū), and Sindhi هَنجَ (hañja).

senses.g: Miss – the title of an unmarried woman

آنکھ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi آن٘کھ (ānkh) (c. 1503) / اَن٘کھ (ankh) (c. 1565), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀺 (akkhi), from Sanskrit अक्षि (ákṣi). Cognates Cognates include Assamese আখি (akhi), Bengali আঁখি (ãkhi), Bhojpuri आँखि (ā̃khi), Gujarati આંખ (ā̃kha), Kashmiri أچھ / ॳछ (ạch), Maithili आँखि (ā̃khɨ), Odia ଆଖି (ākhi), Punjabi اَکّھ (akkh), Romani jakh, and Sindhi اَکہِ (akhi).

آنگن.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀕𑀡 (aṃgaṇa), from Sanskrit अङ्गन (aṅgana).

senses.g: an anna (¹⁄₁₆ of an Indian rupee)
Awaran (a city in Awaran district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

آواز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آواز (āvāz), from Middle Persian ʾwʾz (āwāz). Cognate with Sanskrit वाच् (vā́c) and Latin vōx. First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi آواز (āvaz).

senses.g: the arch of a cupola or dome

آہنگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آهنگ (āhang), from Middle Persian ʾʾhng (āhang, “pull, draught; melody”).

senses.g: masculine singular perfect adjectival of آنا (ānā): came, arrived

آیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀆𑀕𑀤 (āgada) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit आगत (ā́gata). Cognate with Punjabi ਆਇਆ (āiā) / آیا, Marathi आले (āle).

آیا صوفیہ.e: Borrowed either from Persian ایاصوفیه (ayâ sofiye) or Arabic آيا صوفيا (Āyā Ṣūfiyā), ultimately from Byzantine Greek Ἁγία Σοφία (Hagía Sophía).

آیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آیَه (āya), from Arabic آيَة (ʔāya).

آیت اللہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آیَتُاللّٰه (āyatu-l-lāh), in turn borrowed from Arabic آيَة اللّٰه (āyat allāh).

senses.g: Iceland (an island and country in the North Atlantic Ocean in Europe)
in the future, as we move ahead
age, duration or period of a lifetime
inflection of آنا (ānā): — feminine singular perfective participle
inflection of آنا (ānā): — feminine singular perfect indicative
Ivory Coast (a country in West Africa)
The second letter of the Urdu alphabet, called بے (be) and written in the Arabic script.

بابت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَابَت (bābat), borrowed from Arabic بَابَة (bāba).

senses.g: in the matter of, on account of, as regards, as for

بابت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَابَت (bābat), borrowed from Arabic بَابَة (bāba).

senses.g: Term of address for a little boy or one's son.

بابو.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀩𑀸𑀩𑁆𑀩- (*bābba-), imitative of baby talk.

senses.g: sign (ie. an indication of something that's being alluded to)

ex.u: میں کل میرے بھائی سے بات کر رہا تھا۔
ex.r: ma͠i kal mere bhāī se bāt kar rahā thā.
ex.e: Yesterday I was talking with my brother.

باتچیت.e: Compound of بات (bāt, “talk, matter”) + چیت* (*cīt), a term found only in this compound, from Prakrit 𑀘𑀺𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺 (citti), from Sanskrit चित्ति (citti, “thought”). Cognate to Gujarati વાતચીત (vātcīt).

senses.g: oblique plural of بات (bāt, “talk; conversation; matter”)
direct plural of بات (bāt, “talk; conversation; matter”)

باٹ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀝𑁆𑀝 (vaṭṭa), from Sanskrit वर्त्मन् (vártman), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *wártma, from Proto-Indo-European *wért-mn̥, from Proto-Indo-European *wert-.

senses.g: orchestra (a group or entity which creates music through instruments)

باجا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀚𑁆𑀚 (vajja), from Sanskrit वाद्य (vādya), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *wā́dyam, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *wā́dyam.

باجی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاجِی (bājī), from Ottoman Turkish باجی (bacı).

بادامی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بادامی (bādāmī). By surface analysis, بادام (bādām) + ـی (-ī).

بادشاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بادشاه (bādšāh), variant of Classical Persian پادشاه (pādišāh). Compare Maithili बादसाह (bādᵊsāh) / 𑒥𑒰𑒠𑒮𑒰𑒯 (bādasāha), Punjabi ਬਾਦਸ਼ਾਹ (bādśāh) / بادْشاہ (bādśāh), Sindhi بادشاهُ (bādśāhu) / बादशाहु, Gujarati બાદશાહ (bādśāh), Marathi बादशहा (bādaśhā), Bengali বাদশাহ (badośah), Telugu పాదుషా (pāduṣā).

senses.g: reign; the rank or office of a badshah or padishah

بادشاہت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بادشاهت (“royalty”). By surface analysis, بَادْشَاہ (bādśāh, “king, monarch”) + َت (at).

بادل.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بادل (badl /⁠bādal⁠/) (c. 1503) / بدل (bdl /⁠badal⁠/) (c. 1434), from Prakrit 𑀯𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀮 (vaddala), from Sanskrit वार्दल (vārdala).

بادنجان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بادنجان from Sanskrit वातिङ्गण (vātiṅgaṇa, “the plant that cures the wind”) from Dravidian, ultimately from Proto-Dravidian *waẓVtV. Doublet of بینگن (baiṅgan).

senses.g: again and again, over and over, time and again, time after time
issue, raining, shower (the descend or advent upon something or someone)
Barkhan (a city in Barkhan district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

باروت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَارُوت (bārūt), from Ottoman Turkish باروت (bârut), from Arabic بَارُود (bārūd). Doublet of بَارُود (bārūd).

بارود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَارُود (bārūd), from Arabic بَارُود (bārūd). Doublet of بَارُوت (bārūt).

بارہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀭𑀲 (bārasa), from Sanskrit द्वादश (dvā́daśa).

senses.g: again and again, over and over, time and again, time after time

A market where foreign goods (especially smuggled items) are available, named after a place called Bāṛā in the northern tribal areas of Pakistan, which was originally famous among buyers for such goods.

باڑھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀟𑁆𑀠𑀺 (vaḍḍhi), from Sanskrit वृद्धि (vṛddhi, “increase”)

ex.u: اس کے بازو پر ایک نشان ہے۔
ex.e: He has a scar on his arm.

senses.g: side of a framework or a door, doorpost

بازو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَازُو (bāzū). Doublet of بَان٘ہہ (bā̃h).

بازیچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَازِیچَه (bāzīča). By surface analysis, بازی (bāzī, “game”) + ـچَہ (-ca), essentially a diminutive of بازی (bāzī, “game; play”).

باسن.e: First attested in c. 1700 as Middle Hindi باسن (bāsan), inherited from Sanskrit वासन (vāsana). Cognate with Gujarati વાસણ (vāsaṇ) and Bengali বাসন (baśon).

باسی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi باسی (basy) (c. 1672), borrowed from Sanskrit वासिन् (vāsin).

باعث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian باعث (bā'is), from Arabic بَاعِث (bāʕiṯ).

باغی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاغِی (bāġī), from Arabic بَاغٍ (bāḡin).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاغِی (bāġī), from Arabic بَاغٍ (bāḡin).

senses.g: grown in plantation or a garden.

باغی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاغِی (bāġī). By surface analysis, بَاغ (bāġ, “garden”) + ـِی (-ī, “-like”).

باغیچہ.e: Derived from Classical Persian باغچه (bāġča), diminutive of باغ (bāġ).

باقی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاقِی (bāqī), from Arabic بَاقٍ (bāqin), from the root ب ق ي (b q y).

ex.e: I will throw away the rest.

باقی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاقِی (bāqī), from Arabic بَاقٍ (bāqin), from the root ب ق ي (b q y).

باکرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian باکره (bākira), from Arabic بَاكِرَة (bākira).

senses.g: Baku (the capital city of the Republic of Azerbaijan)

باگھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀕𑁆𑀖 (vaggha), from Sanskrit व्याघ्र (vyāghra).

بال.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀮 (vāla), from Sanskrit वाल (vāla).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بال (bāl), from Middle Persian b’lk’ (bālag, “wing”).

senses.g: immediate family, parents and their children

بالٹی.e: Borrowed from Portuguese balde. Influence by English bucket.

بالش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَالِش (bāliš), from Middle Persian 𐭡𐭠𐭫𐭱𐭭 (bʾlšn' /⁠bālišn⁠/), from Proto-Iranian *barjíš, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰarȷ́ʰíš, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰelǵʰ-.

بالغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَالِغ (bāliġ), from Arabic بَالِغ (bāliḡ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بَالِغ (bāliġ), from Arabic بَالِغ (bāliḡ).

senses.g: supposing; for the sake of the argument; for instance

بالفرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِالْفَرْض (bi-l-farz), from Arabic فَرْض (farḍ).

senses.g: presently; now; in the current moment

بالفعل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِالْفِعْل (bi-lfi'l), from Arabic بِٱلْفِعْلِ (bi-l-fiʕli).

بالکل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِالْکُلّ (bi-l-kull), from Arabic بِٱلْكُلّ (bi-l-kull).

بالم.e: Inherited from Sanskrit वल्लभतम (vallabhatama). Cognate with Gujarati વાલમ (vālam).

senses.g: a kind of earring with pendants

بانٹنا.e: Urdu verb set بنٹنا بانٹنا بانٹانا Etymology tree Sanskrit वण्टति (vaṇṭati) Prakrit 𑀯𑀁𑀝𑀇 (vaṃṭaï) Urdu بانٹنا Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀁𑀝𑀇 (vaṃṭaï), from Sanskrit वण्टति (vaṇṭati).

senses.g: to read, to read through, to decipher

بانچنا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit वाचयति (vācayati, “causes to speak”). Compare Konkani वाजुचे (vājuce), वाच्चे (vācce), Gujarati વાંચવું (vā̃cvũ), Marathi वाचणे (vācṇe), Punjabi ਵਾਚਣਾ (vācṇā) / واچݨا (vācṇā).

باندھنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀩𑀁𑀥𑀇 (baṃdhaï), from Sanskrit बन्ध॑ति (bándhati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰándʰati, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéndʰ-e-ti, from *bʰendʰ- (“to bind, tie”). Cognate with Punjabi بَنّھݨا (bannhṇā) / ਬੰਨ੍ਹਣਾ (bannhṇā), Saraiki ٻَنھَݨ (ḇanhaṇ), Sindhi ٻَنڌَڻُ (ḇãdhaṇu) / ॿंधणु, Marathi बांधणे (bāndhṇe), English bind.

بانس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀁𑀲 (vaṃsa), from Sanskrit वंश (vaṃśa), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *wanćás.

senses.g: bansuri, a type of flute made from bamboo

بانسری.e: Borrowed from Braj बाँसुरी (bā̃surī). Displaced بَانسْلِی (bānslī).

بانو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بانو (bānū, “lady; queen”).

بانہہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀳𑀸 (bāhā), from Sanskrit बाहु (bāhu), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰāǵʰus. Doublet of بَازُو (bāzū).

بانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَانِی (bānī), borrowed from Arabic بَانٍ (bānin), from بَنَى (banā), from Proto-Semitic *banay- (“to build”), from Proto-Afroasiatic.

باورچی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَاوَرْچِی (bāwarčī). Compare Bengali বাবুর্চি (baburci).

ex.e: to the last grain, absolutely precisely

باون.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀯𑀡 (bāvaṇa), 𑀩𑀸𑀯𑀡𑁆𑀡 (bāvaṇṇa), from Sanskrit द्वापञ्चाशत् (dvāpañcāśat).

باہر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀳𑀺𑀭 (bāhira), from Sanskrit बहिर् (bahir), a compounding form of बहिस् (bahis).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀳𑀺𑀭 (bāhira), from Sanskrit बहिर् (bahir), a compounding form of बहिस् (bahis).

senses.g: An employee of a teahouse or restaurant who delivers tea or food to offices, shops, etc.; a waiter assigned to deliver items outside the restaurant.

باہم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian با هم (bā-ham).

senses.g: Bahubali, a Jain deity, son of Rishabhanatha.
a male given name, equivalent to English Bahubali

بائیس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀩𑀸𑀯𑀻𑀲 (bāvīsa), from Sanskrit द्वाविंशति (dvā́viṃśati).

بآواز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَآوَاز (baāwāz), from بـ (b-, “with”) + آواز (āvāz, “audio; sound”).

بباد.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit विवाद (vivādá). Doublet of وِواد (vivād)

ببھکشا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit बुभुक्षा (bubhukṣā). Doublet of بُھوک (bhūk).

senses.g: baptism (a Christian sacrament, by which one is received into a church and sometimes given a name, generally involving the candidate to be anointed with or submerged in water)

ex.u: یہ پانی اس بپتسمہ کی طرف اشارہ ہے جو اس وقت آپ کو نجات دلاتا ہے۔ اس سے جسم کی گندگی دور نہیں کی جاتی بلکہ بپتسمہ لیتے وقت ہم خدا سے عرض کرتے ہیں کہ وہ ہمارا ضمیر پاک صاف کردے۔ پھر یہ آپ کو یسوع مسیح کے جی اٹھنے سے نجات دلاتا ہے۔
ex.r: yah pānī us baptismā kī taraf iśārā hai jo is vaqt āp ko najāt dilātā hai. is se jism kī gandagī dūr nahī̃ kī jātī balki baptismā lete vaqt ham xudā se arz karte ha͠i ki voh hamārā zamīr pāk-sāf kar de. phir yah āp ko yesu masīh ke jī uṭhne se najāt dilātā hai.
ex.e: And that water is like baptism, which now saves you. Baptism is not the washing of dirt from the body. It is asking God for a clean conscience. It saves you because Jesus Christ was raised from death.

بپتسمہ.e: Borrowed from Portuguese baptismo or English baptism.

بت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بت (bat, “mark, score”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بت (but), from Middle Persian [script needed] (bwt' /⁠but⁠/, “Buddha, idol”), ultimately from Sanskrit बुद्ध (buddha). Doublet of بدھ (buddh).

بت پرست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بتپرست (but-parast). By surface analysis, بت (but, “idol”) + پرست (-parast, “worshiper, follower, -ist”).

بت پرستی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بتپرستی (but-parastī). Equivalent to بت پرست (but-parast) + ـی (-ī).

بت خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بتخانه (but-xāna), from بت (but, “idol”) + خانه (xāna, “house”).

senses.g: a title, famously applied to Mahmud Ghaznavi and Hazrat Ali

بت شکن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بت شکن (but-šikan, “iconoclast”). By surface analysis, بُت (but, “idol”) + شکن (śikan, “breaker, smasher”), literally “idol-breaker”.

بت کدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بتکده (but-kada), from بت (but, “idol”) + کده (kada, “temple”).

بتانا.e: From Sanskrit वत् (vat) + ـآ (-ā, causative augment) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

senses.g: to spend, pass, live out (said of time, life etc.)
A ball not properly bowled with a correct arm rotation; an illegally delivered ball. In formal cricket terminology, this is called a throw.

ex.u: بٹّا بال پر ہم آؤٹ نہیں دیں گے۔
ex.r: baṭṭā bāl par hum out nahī deinge.
ex.e: We won't give you out on a throw.

بٹا بال.e: From Urdu بَٹّا (defect; flaw; fault) + English ball (a cricket ball).

senses.g: to collect, to gather, to accumulate

بجلی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀼𑀮𑀺𑀆 (vijjuliā), from Sanskrit विद्युल्लता (vidyullatā, “forked lightning”), from विद्युत् (vidyut, “lightning”) + लता (latā, “creeper”). Cognate with Odia ବିଜୁଳି (bijuḷi), Gujarati વીજળી (vījaḷī), Nepali बिजुली (bijulī), Assamese বিজুলী (bizuli), Punjabi ਬਿਜਲੀ (bijlī), Sindhi بجلي (bjle).

senses.g: to sound; make noise (of an alarm etc.)

بجے.e: Plural perfective participle of بجنا ("to strike").

بچانا.e: Cognate to Punjabi ਬਚਾਉਣਾ (bacāuṇā) / بَچاؤݨا (bacā'oṇā), Marathi वाचवणे (vāċavṇe). Urdu verb set بچنا بچانا

بچنا.e: Urdu verb set بچنا بچانا Etymology tree Proto-Indo-European *wenk- Proto-Indo-European *wn̥k-yé-tor Proto-Indo-Iranian *wačyátay Proto-Indo-Aryan *waćyátay Sanskrit व॒च्यते॑ (vacyáte) Proto-Indo-European *wreg- Proto-Indo-European *-eti Proto-Indo-European *wrégeti Proto-Indo-Iranian *wráǰati Sanskrit व्रज॑ति (vrájati)? Prakrit 𑀯𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀇 (vaccaï) Urdu بچنا Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀇 (vaccaï, “to move”), from Sanskrit व॒च्यते॑ (vacyáte, “to move to and fro”), or possibly from Sanskrit व्रज॑ति (vrájati, “to go”).

بچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَچَّه (bačča), from Middle Persian wck' (waččag, “child, baby, cub”), from Proto-Iranian *wacáh (“calf”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *watsás.
Borrowed from Classical Persian بَچَّه (bačča), from Middle Persian wck' (waččag, “child, baby, cub”), from Proto-Iranian *wacáh (“calf”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *watsás.

senses.g: feminine of بَچَّہ (bacca); girl, female child
inflection of بَچَّہ (bacca, “kid; boy”): — direct plural
inflection of بَچَّہ (bacca, “kid; boy”): — oblique/vocative singular

بچھانا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀯𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀙𑀸𑀤𑁂𑀤𑀺 (*vicchādedi), from Sanskrit विच्छादयति (vicchādayati, “uncovers, unclothes”).

بحث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بحث (bahs, “discussion, argument”), from Arabic بَحْث (baḥṯ, “quest; examination”).

بحر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بحر (bahr), from Arabic بَحْر (baḥr).

بحر الکاہل.e: Literally “the indolent ocean”, from بَحْر (bahr, “sea, ocean”) + کاہِل (kāhil, “lazy, indolent”).

بحر اوقیانوس.e: Literally “ocean of the ocean”, from بَحْر (bahr, “sea, ocean”) + اُوقِیَانُوس (ūqiyānūs, “ocean”), from Arabic بَحْر (baḥr) and أُوقْيَانُوس (ʔūqyānūs).

بحر منجمد جنوبی.e: Literally “southern frozen ocean”, from بَحْر (bahr, “sea, ocean”) + مُنْجَمِد (munjamid, “frozen, congealed”) + جُنُوبی (junūbī, “south”), a partial calque of Persian اقیانوس منجمد جنوبی (oqyânus-e monjamed-e janubi).

بحر منجمد شمالی.e: Literally “northern frozen ocean”, from بَحْر (bahr, “sea, ocean”) + مُنْجَمِد (munjamid, “frozen, congealed”) + شُمالی (śumālī, “north”), a partial calque of Persian اقیانوس منجمد شمالی (oqyânus-e monjamed-e šomâli).

بحر ہند.e: From بَحْر (bahr, “sea, ocean”) + ہِنْد (hind, “India”). Compare Persian اقیانوس هند (oqyânus-e hend).

بحران.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُحْرَان (buhrān), from Arabic بُحْرَان (buḥrān).

senses.g: Bahrain (an archipelago, island, and country in West Asia in the Persian Gulf)
Bahrain (a town in Swat district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

بحرین.e: Borrowed from Arabic الْبَحْرَيْن (al-baḥrayn), possibly influenced by Persian بحرین (bahreyn).

بخار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُخَار (buxār), borrowed from Arabic بُخَار (buḵār).

بخیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَخِیْل (baxiyl), from Arabic بَخِيل (baḵīl).

بد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بد (bad). Unrelated to English bad.

بد اخلاق.e: From Classical Persian بد اخلاق (bad axlāq).

ex.u: تمہاری بغلوں سے بدبو آ رہی ہے
ex.r: tumhārī baġalõ se bad-bū ā rhī he!
ex.e: there is a stench coming off your armpits!

بد بو.e: From Classical Persian بَد بو (bad-bū, “stink, stench”).

بد بودار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَد بُودَار (bad būdār, “stinky, smelly”), analyzable as بد (bad, “bad, disagreeable”) + بو (bū, “smell, odor”) + دار (dār, “having”).
